title: Failed one anti-TNF? Second-line vedolizumab still made UC patients 7.13× to 11.35× more likely to hit remission
paper: Lukas M et al. Ther Adv Gastroenterol. 2026 (PMID: 41883697)
finding: A meta-analysis of patient-level data from GEMINI-1 and VISIBLE-1 found that after failure of one anti-TNF, second-line vedolizumab significantly improved week-52 outcomes versus placebo. Patients were 7.13× to 11.35× more likely to achieve clinical remission, with better steroid-free remission, endoscopic improvement, and quality of life.

title: Stopping mesalamine backfires: relapse risk jumps 60% with oral 5-ASA and doubles with rectal therapy
paper: Arzivian A et al. Inflamm Bowel Dis. 2026 Apr 1 (PMID: 41338243)
finding: A systematic review and meta-analysis of 29 studies found that stopping oral 5-ASA monotherapy in quiescent UC raised relapse risk by 60% (RR 1.60), while stopping rectal 5-ASA doubled relapse risk (RR 2.03). In contrast, stopping 5-ASA after escalation to immunomodulators or biologics did not clearly increase relapse risk, suggesting monitored withdrawal may be reasonable only in selected patients.

title: Oral S1P drugs more than tripled maintenance response in UC, new meta-analysis finds
paper: Gardezi SAA et al. Dig Dis Sci. 2026 Apr 13 (PMID: 41968221)
finding: A systematic review and meta-analysis of 8 randomized trials (2,663 patients) found S1P receptor modulators significantly improved every major UC outcome versus placebo. Maintenance clinical response was 3.30× higher, clinical remission 2.20× higher, histological remission 2.60× higher, and mucosal healing 2.21× higher. Serious adverse events and discontinuations were not increased.

title: FMT works best by colonoscopy + enemas: 2.39× more remissions in UC, updated meta-analysis finds
paper: Huang B et al. Adv Ther. 2026 Apr 7 (PMID: 41945234)
finding: Updated meta-analysis of 16 RCTs found FMT improved clinical remission overall (RR 1.81) and endoscopic remission (RR 1.74) without increasing adverse events. The strongest signal was route-dependent: colonoscopy plus enema delivery produced 2.39× more clinical remissions and 2.19× more endoscopic remissions than control, outperforming single-route approaches.

title: TL1A inhibitors: the next biologic class that targets both UC inflammation AND intestinal scarring — Phase 3 programs now underway
paper: Goyal MK et al. Aliment Pharmacol Ther. 2026 Apr 6 (PMID: 41941212)
finding: A comprehensive narrative synthesis of TL1A/DR3 biology and clinical evidence confirms that TL1A inhibitors (tulisokibart, duvakitug, afimkibart) represent a mechanistically distinct biologic class uniquely capable of blocking both the inflammatory cascade AND intestinal fibrosis in IBD. Tulisokibart Phase 2 (ARTEMIS-UC) showed 26% clinical remission vs 1% placebo. Phase 3 programs are now actively enrolling. Unlike all existing UC biologics, TL1A inhibitors target the inflammation-fibrosis continuum — directly relevant to the ~30% of UC patients who develop intestinal strictures.

title: IL-23 inhibitors: 2.53× more mucosal healing AND 61% fewer serious side effects — meta-analysis of 7 RCTs settles the class debate
paper: Amin HM et al. Int J Colorectal Dis. 2025 Dec 30;41(1):1 (PMID: 41467943)
finding: A systematic review and meta-analysis of 7 RCTs (4,203 patients) found IL-23 inhibitors (mirikizumab, risankizumab, guselkumab) deliver histo-endoscopic mucosal healing at 2.53× the rate of placebo during induction and 1.81× at maintenance, with clinical remission RR 1.52 (induction) and RR 1.62 (maintenance). Most striking: serious adverse events were 61% LOWER with IL-23 inhibitors vs placebo during induction (RR 0.39). Effective disease control reduces the disease-driven adverse events that dominate the placebo arm.

title: Etrasimod: 6.93× more mucosal healing at 40 weeks — first GRADE meta-analysis of the oral S1P pill for UC
paper: Jha A et al. Ann Pharmacother. 2026 Mar 27 (PMID: 41891290)
finding: GRADE-based meta-analysis of 4 RCTs (1239 patients) confirmed etrasimod's maintenance power: clinical remission RR 4.28 at 40+ weeks (I²=0%), endoscopic improvement RR 3.51, mucosal healing RR 6.93 (I²=0%), steroid-free remission RR 4.28 vs placebo. Induction: clinical remission RR 2.92, response RR 1.71, mucosal healing RR 3.11. No excess serious adverse events. Zero heterogeneity in all maintenance endpoints — exceptionally consistent signal.

title: Appendicectomy beat JAK inhibitors 3:1 in biologic-refractory UC — landmark Lancet 2026 trial
paper: Visser E et al. (COSTA Study Group). Lancet Gastroenterol Hepatol. 2026 Mar;11(3):190-203 (PMID: 41512889)
finding: In the COSTA multicentre prospective cohort (116 biologic-exposed active UC patients), laparoscopic appendicectomy achieved 32.8% clinical remission at 12 months vs 12.2% with switching to a JAK inhibitor (p=0.010). Clinical response 73.1% vs 53.1%; endoscopic response 48.4% vs 25.6%. Colectomy rates were similar (9% vs 8.2%). Procedure was safe with only 3 grade ≤II complications.

title: Mirikizumab: 77.7% still in clinical remission at 4 years — longest IL-23 durability data in UC
paper: Sands BE et al. Inflamm Bowel Dis. 2026 Mar (PMID: 41777218)
finding: In the LUCENT-3 open-label extension (868 patients, 835 patient-years), mirikizumab W52 remitters maintained 77.7% clinical remission, 81.3% endoscopic remission, and 94.3% symptomatic remission at 4 years (week 212). W52 responders showed 68.7% clinical and 70.2% endoscopic remission at 4 years. No new safety signals over 160+ weeks of long-term exposure.

title: 50% remission in 8 weeks: Curcumin + QingDai beat placebo in active UC RCT
paper: Ben-Horin S et al. Clin Gastroenterol Hepatol. 2024 (PMID: 37302449)
finding: In a randomized double-blind placebo-controlled trial of active UC, CurQD achieved 85.7% clinical response vs 30.7% placebo, 50% clinical remission vs 8% placebo, and 75% endoscopic improvement vs 20% by week 8.

title: Mediterranean diet + curcumin reduced active UC disease activity in 8 weeks — multicenter RCT
paper: Erol Doğan Ö et al. Nutrients. 2024 (PMID: 38794742)
finding: A 3-arm multicenter RCT (48 patients with confirmed mild-to-moderate active UC on mesalamine) found that Mediterranean diet alone, MD + 1,600 mg/day curcumin, and MD + resveratrol all significantly reduced Truelove–Witts disease activity scores, serum inflammatory markers, and improved quality of life (all p < 0.05) in 8 weeks. The diet alone matched supplemented arms on most endpoints.

title: FMT nearly triples the odds of UC remission — meta-analysis of 15 RCTs confirms gut bacteria transplant works
paper: Doukas PG et al. Dig Dis (Basel). 2026 (PMID: 41078065)
finding: Fecal microbiota transplantation achieved 62% clinical remission vs 50.5% in controls (OR 2.65; 95% CI 1.76–4.00) across 15 randomised trials. Endoscopic response nearly doubled: 42% FMT vs 22% placebo (OR 2.00). Low heterogeneity — a consistent, robust signal. Non-pharmacological, no systemic immunosuppression.

title: JAK inhibitors: 86.5% clinical response in the worst UC flares within 30 days — meta-analysis of 35 studies
paper: Jena A et al. Clin Gastroenterol Hepatol. 2026 (PMID: 41690364)
finding: Upadacitinib achieved 86.5% short-term clinical response in acute severe UC hospitalised patients within 30 days (95% CI 72.3–94.1%), with colectomy rates of just 11.2%. Tofacitinib achieved 77.9% response. Meta-analysis of 35 studies, 664 patients. Both drugs outperform historical rescue therapy benchmarks.

title: Anti-inflammatory diet: 82% better clinical response in active UC — meta-analysis of 6 RCTs
paper: Rehmat et al. Dig Dis Sci. 2026 (PMID: 40797000)
finding: Whole-food anti-inflammatory diets increased clinical response in active UC by 82% (RR 1.82, p=0.004, I²=0%) and reduced fecal calprotectin by 210 µg/g during remission (p=0.006). Effect was on top of standard medication across 359 patients in 6 RCTs.

title: ACCURE RCT: Appendectomy reduces UC relapse by 35% — first surgical RCT for UC
paper: ACCURE Trial. Lancet Gastro Hepatol. 2025
finding: Therapeutic appendectomy + standard therapy reduced 12-month relapse to 33% vs >50% with standard therapy alone (RR 0.65). Earlier meta-analyses showed OR 0.31 for UC risk when appendectomy performed before age 20.

title: Guselkumab (anti-IL-23): >80% sustained clinical remission at 3 years
paper: QUASAR/ASTRO Extension. FDA approved Sept 2025
finding: Guselkumab achieved 27.6% induction remission and >80% sustained remission at 3 years — the strongest durability signal among IL-23 inhibitors. Now FDA-approved for UC.

title: 2025 NMA (36 RCTs, 14,270 patients): Upadacitinib ranked #1 for UC remission
paper: Shehab et al. 2025 Network Meta-Analysis
finding: Upadacitinib ranked first for clinical remission (34% induction, 54% maintenance), endoscopic improvement, and histological remission across 36 RCTs. Symptom relief from day 1.

title: VEGA: Dual biologic therapy (guselkumab + golimumab) hits 83% response
paper: VEGA Trial
finding: Combining guselkumab + golimumab achieved 83% clinical response vs 61-75% with monotherapy. Dual targeted therapy may break the ~50% monotherapy remission ceiling.

title: Tulisokibart (anti-TL1A): 26% vs 1% remission — first precision medicine UC drug
paper: ARTEMIS-UC Phase 2 (Merck). Phase 3 ATLAS-UC underway
finding: First UC therapy with a companion diagnostic to identify responders. TL1A regulates both inflammation and fibrosis, suggesting potential disease-modifying properties beyond symptom control.

title: Red and processed meat: OR 5.19 for UC relapse in highest consumption tertile
paper: Prospective cohort + dose-response meta-analysis
finding: The single most impactful dietary trigger. Sulfur amino acids metabolized into hydrogen sulfide — directly toxic to colonocytes and impairs butyrate oxidation. UC patients already have elevated colonic H2S.

title: Air pollution (PM2.5): Mendelian randomization suggests OR 3.6 for UC risk
paper: UK Biobank (455,210 participants); MR analysis
finding: PM2.5, PM10, NO2 all significantly increased UC risk (notably not Crohns). Mechanism: particles reach colon via mucociliary clearance, disrupt tight junctions, induce oxidative stress.

title: Multi-strain probiotics: OR 3.85 for remission induction (NMA of 20 RCTs)
paper: Network Meta-Analysis of 20 RCTs
finding: Lactobacillus + Bifidobacterium combinations achieved OR 3.85 vs placebo. VSL#3/Visbiome (8-strain) showed 42.9% vs 15.7% remission. Probiotics 66% lower relapse in maintenance (OR 0.34).

title: FMT + anti-inflammatory diet hit 3.5× higher clinical response than standard therapy in mild-moderate UC
finding: Combining multidonor FMT with an anti-inflammatory diet produced 60% remission and 36.4% deep remission at 8 weeks. Diet alone then maintained deep remission in 25% of patients at 48 weeks versus 0% in the standard-therapy group.

title: PREdiCCt: Faecal calprotectin ≥250 predicts objective flare with aHR 3.25
paper: PREdiCCt cohort. Gut 2025. PMID: 41554630
finding: In the largest prospective IBD flare-prediction cohort, FC ≥250 µg/g vs <50 predicted objective UC flare with aHR 3.25 — even in patients reporting remission.

title: Mesalamine non-adherence multiplies relapse risk by 5.5×
paper: Kane et al. Am J Med. 2003. PMID: 12543288
finding: Prospective data showed non-adherent UC patients had HR 5.5 for relapse — making medication adherence the single highest-leverage modifiable factor.

title: AGA 2024 Living Guideline: early advanced therapy for moderate-to-severe UC
paper: AGA Clinical Practice Guideline. Gastroenterology. Dec 2024
finding: AGA now recommends initiating advanced therapy early rather than prolonged step-up delays after 5-ASA failure, with structured monitoring timelines.

title: BSG 2025: Comprehensive adult IBD guideline with treat-to-target monitoring
paper: British Society of Gastroenterology. Gut 2025
finding: BSG pragmatic thresholds: FC <100 likely endoscopic remission; FC >200 low likelihood. Symptoms by 3 months, biomarkers 3–6 months, endoscopy 6–12 months.

title: PREdiCCt: Highest meat intake quartile doubles objective flare risk
paper: PREdiCCt cohort. Gut 2025. PMID: 41554630
finding: Highest vs lowest quartile total meat intake in UC was associated with aHR 1.95 for objective flare — one of the strongest dietary signals.

title: High perceived stress linked to 3.6× higher odds of UC flare
paper: Sauk et al. Clin Gastroenterol Hepatol. 2023
finding: Stress was the #1 patient-reported flare trigger (37.9% of 27,000+ forum posts), and prospective data showed 3.6× flare odds with high perceived stress.

title: Cochrane: Curcumin adjunct reduces UC relapse to 4% vs 18% (RR 0.24)
paper: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews
finding: Curcumin 2g/day as add-on to mesalamine cut 6-month relapse from 18% to 4%. ECCO/ESPGHAN now recognises it as a potential additional therapy.

title: FMT meta-analysis: OR 2.25 for combined clinical + endoscopic remission
paper: Meta-analysis of 14 RCTs. PMID: 35766805
finding: Multi-donor FMT (OR 3.32) and oral delivery (OR 3.15) showed best results. AGA recommends FMT for UC only in clinical trial settings (2024).

title: Antivirals in CMV+ steroid-refractory UC reduce colectomy odds by 80%
paper: Meta-analysis. PMID: 29392650
finding: Antiviral therapy in CMV-positive steroid-refractory UC associated with colectomy OR 0.20 — a dramatic reduction in surgical need.

title: Infliximab established anti-TNF induction and maintenance benefit in ACT 1 and ACT 2
paper: Rutgeerts et al. N Engl J Med. 2005. PMID: 16339095
finding: Infliximab delivered roughly 30-35% remission at week 8 in moderate to severe UC and became a benchmark rescue and maintenance biologic.

title: U-ACHIEVE: Upadacitinib delivers 26–34% remission at week 8 (NNT ≈ 3–5)
paper: Danese et al. Lancet. 2022. PMID: 35644166
finding: Upadacitinib reached 74% response and 26% remission at week 8, with maintenance at 42–52% vs 12% — one of the strongest oral signals in UC.

title: GEMINI 1: Vedolizumab gut-selective approach with 42% remission at week 52
paper: Feagan et al. N Engl J Med. 2013. PMID: 23964932
finding: Vedolizumab produced about 17% remission at week 6 and around 42% remission at week 52 with a favorable safety profile.

title: LUCENT: Mirikizumab maintenance remission 49.9% at week 40
paper: D'Haens et al. Lancet. 2023
finding: Mirikizumab produced about 63% response with 24% remission at week 12 and 49.9% maintenance remission — strongest IL-23 maintenance signal.

title: OCTAVE: Tofacitinib oral JAK inhibitor with rapid symptom control
paper: Sandborn et al. N Engl J Med. 2017. PMID: 28467869
finding: Tofacitinib reached about 59% response and 19% remission at week 8, with maintenance at 34–41% vs 11%.

title: UNIFI validated IL-12/23 blockade for ulcerative colitis
paper: Sands et al. N Engl J Med. 2019. PMID: 31553833
finding: Ustekinumab showed about 62% response with roughly 16% remission at week 8 in moderate to severe disease.

title: TRUE NORTH: Ozanimod S1P modulation for moderate-to-severe UC
paper: Sandborn et al. N Engl J Med. 2021. PMID: 34587385
finding: Ozanimod delivered about 18% remission at week 10 and 37% maintenance among responders (NNT ≈ 5).

title: ELEVATE: Etrasimod confirmed as second oral S1P option
paper: Sandborn et al. Lancet. 2023. PMID: 36871574
finding: Etrasimod showed around 53% response at week 12 (NNT ≈ 10 induction) and expanded the S1P class after 2023 approval.

title: Vitamin D supplementation reduces IBD relapse (RR 0.64) — UC subgroup needs more data
paper: RCT meta-analysis. PMID: 36579768
finding: Across IBD, vitamin D supplementation reduced relapse RR to 0.64. UC-specific evidence remains underpowered but 45–50% of UC patients are deficient.
